target.title: feat(approvals): add plain-language plugin approvals
target.body:
## Mission

Plugin approvals are a security boundary, but they are often delivered in chat where the old fallback text read like internal debug output. A normal approval could show a raw title, full command text, proposed exec policy, session key, tool/plugin IDs, expiry, and `/approve <id>` syntax all at once.

That raw format is useful for maintainers, but it is not easy for a Telegram/Matrix user to scan quickly. The goal of this PR is to make opt-in plugin approvals understandable at a glance while preserving the legacy raw behavior by default.

In short: keep the original technical approval prompt as the default, and add a plain-language approval mode for users who explicitly choose it.

## What This Adds

This PR adds `approvals.plugin.language` with three modes:

- `original`: existing raw technical plugin approval text. This remains the default.
- `simple`: a short plain-language prompt with `Action`, `It will`, `Risk`, and available choices.
- `simple-technical`: the plain-language prompt plus the raw technical fields below it.

Example config:

```json
{
  "approvals": {
    "plugin": {
      "language": "simple"
    }
  }
}
```

The new renderer keeps interactive approval metadata intact so native Telegram/Matrix approval buttons can still be rendered. Text-only channels still get an actionable `/approve plugin:<id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` fallback.

## Implementation

- Added the typed `PluginApprovalLanguage` config surface and validation.
- Wired the configured language through shared plugin approval rendering.
- Forwarded the language option through fallback delivery and native Telegram/Matrix approval payloads.
- Added plain-English approval output with risk text and decision-aware fallback instructions.
- Added `Command preview` output for commands that are high risk or cannot be summarized completely.
- Redacted token/password/API-key/bearer/basic-auth/cookie/userinfo forms from previews before they can be posted into chat.
- Updated generated config and plugin SDK API baselines.
- Added focused renderer and forwarder tests.

## Safety Model

The important rule is: if `simple` mode hides raw technical details, the summary must either explain the meaningful action or fail closed.

Fail closed means the approval becomes higher risk and shows a redacted command preview instead of pretending the command is harmless.

The renderer now handles and tests common Codex app-server command shapes:

- shell wrappers: `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, `zsh -c`, `env`, `timeout`, `set -a`, `source`;
- shell control: `&&`, `||`, `;`, background `&`, conditionals, loops, and attached redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`;
- pipelines: known stages are summarized stage-by-stage; unknown stages become high risk with a preview;
- file reads/writes/deletes: `cat`, `sed`, `find`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, redirection writes;
- network transfers: `curl` and `wget` URL targets, upload operands, output operands, stdin upload via redirection, config/header/cookie/auth/netrc forms;
- credentials: bearer headers, OAuth bearer flags, basic auth, proxy auth, cookie headers, netrc credential sources, URL userinfo;
- dev/ops commands: `git`, `docker`, service managers, package managers, interpreters, logs/process inspection;
- remote commands: `ssh`, `scp`, SSH remote commands, and SSH local-execution options such as `ProxyCommand` and `LocalCommand`.

## Review And Hardening Trail

This PR went through a long approval-safety review loop because turning verbose technical prompts into plain language makes the summarizer security-sensitive.

Issues fixed during review:

- Preserved actionable text fallback instructions for channels without buttons.
- Made pipelines visible before raw command text can be hidden.
- Made command target extraction command-aware so boolean flags like `cat -n .env` do not hide sensitive targets.
- Surfaced curl/wget upload and output operands, including `.env` style local-file transfers.
- Handled stdin uploads such as `curl --data-binary @- ... < .env`.
- Redacted basic-auth, OAuth bearer, cookie, header, and URL credential forms from command previews.
- Failed closed on curl config files and curl header-file inputs.
- Surfaced wget credential/header/cookie options.
- Showed targets for redirected formatter writes like `printf ... > .env`.
- Handled background shell separators such as `sleep 1 & rm -rf /tmp/x`.
- Parsed Git global options such as `git -C repo reset --hard` before classifying subcommands.
- Treated `find` output-file predicates like `-fprint`, `-fprint0`, `-fls`, and `-fprintf` as writes.
- Detected attached shell redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`.
- Failed closed on SSH local-execution options such as `ssh -o ProxyCommand=...` and `ssh -o LocalCommand=...`.
- Refreshed against latest `upstream/main` repeatedly and regenerated the config baseline after conflicts.
- Stabilized cron test mock state exposed by the refreshed CI shard.

The latest ClawSweeper review no longer reports a concrete repair item. It now asks for human maintainer review of the opt-in approval-safety boundary and branch scope.

## Current Status

- Latest head at time of this update: `91d6303a0e`.
- GitHub reports the PR mergeable and clean.
- CI/checks are green on the latest head.
- `Real behavior proof` is successful.
- ClawSweeper durable review status: needs maintainer review before merge, not an automated repair.
- Remaining decision: maintainers need to accept the opt-in plain-language approval UX/security boundary, or ask for scope splitting if they want the unrelated refreshed-base/test-stability churn separated.

## Verification

Local verification after the latest SSH hardening and main refresh:

- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`

Earlier focused verification also covered config schema/help quality, plugin SDK API baseline, gateway/channel state, and live renderer output.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Chat-delivered plugin approvals can now render as a short plain-English approval card instead of a raw technical dump when `approvals.plugin.language` is set to `simple`. The old raw format remains the default through `original`.
- Real environment tested: Hadi's live OpenClaw source checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 (`/home/hadi/src/openclaw`), the PR worktree, and Telegram channel status in polling mode.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rendered real approval messages through `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage(..., { language: "simple" })`.
  - Ran focused approval renderer and forwarder tests.
  - Ran type, lint, config docs, and baseline checks.
  - Cherry-picked the feature into the live source checkout during testing, ran `pnpm build`, restarted `openclaw-gateway.service`, and checked gateway/Telegram channel state.
- Evidence after fix: terminal renderer output showed the intended card shape:

```text
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Create/check workspace files or folders

It will
- create folder(s): memory
- check whether a condition or file exists: memory/context.md

Risk: Low
Only creates or checks workspace files. No network, service, or delete step detected.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:example allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Pipeline and unknown-stage proof:

```text
--- pipeline curl ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Use the network or download data

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- make a network request or download data: https://example.test/upload

Risk: Medium
Network commands can send or fetch data outside this machine.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:pipeline allow-once|allow-always|deny

--- unknown pipeline ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Run a terminal command

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- run custom-uploader

Command preview
cat notes.txt | custom-uploader --send

Risk: High
This command is part of a pipeline I cannot fully summarize, so review it before approving.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:unknown allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Live channel state checked during proof:

```text
Telegram default (Telegram): enabled, configured, running, connected, mode:polling, token:tokenFile
```

Observed result after fix:

- `simple` mode no longer includes the raw technical details block.
- Escaped shell separators such as `&&` are decoded before parsing.
- Shell conditionals no longer surface as fake `run if` / `run then` actions.
- Non-shell pipeline stages are visible or fail closed with a high-risk preview.
- Sensitive command previews are redacted before being shown in chat.
- SSH local-execution options now fail closed with a high-risk preview.

- What was not tested:

- The Mantis Telegram Desktop GIF run skipped instead of producing before/after GIFs. This PR still has terminal renderer proof, live gateway/channel state proof, earlier Telegram screenshot proof, and a successful `Real behavior proof` check.

target.title: perf(terminal): reuse ANSI truncation scanner
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?
- `truncateToVisibleWidth` rebuilt the same combined ANSI/OSC scanner every time a truncation path needed to preserve escape sequences.
- This reuses one module-level scanner and resets `lastIndex` per call so repeated styled/plain truncations remain deterministic.

Why does this matter now?
- Terminal table/string rendering can call truncation repeatedly while formatting CLI output, so avoiding per-call regex allocation keeps this hot helper a little cheaper without changing output behavior.

What is the intended outcome?
- Preserve existing styled truncation behavior while avoiding repeated `RegExp` construction in the truncation path.

What is intentionally out of scope?
- No terminal rendering redesign, width algorithm changes, or broad performance tuning.

What does success look like?
- Repeated styled and plain truncation calls return the same visible strings and widths as before, and focused terminal-core tests stay green.

What should reviewers focus on?
- Whether resetting `lastIndex` before each scan is sufficient for sharing the global regex safely inside this synchronous helper.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
- No direct maintainer request; small external performance cleanup found while reviewing terminal helper hot paths.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: repeated terminal table/string truncation avoids allocating a new combined ANSI regex on each truncation path while preserving styled output.
- Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw worktree, Node v24.9.0, after `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx -e "import { truncateToVisibleWidth, visibleWidth } from './packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.ts'; const first = truncateToVisibleWidth('\\x1b[31mabc\\x1b[0m', 2); const second = truncateToVisibleWidth('plain', 3); const third = truncateToVisibleWidth('\\x1b[32mxy\\x1b[0m', 1); console.log(JSON.stringify({ first, firstWidth: visibleWidth(first), second, secondWidth: visibleWidth(second), third, thirdWidth: visibleWidth(third) }));"`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Console output copied from the patched OpenClaw terminal-core helper run:
  ```json
  {"first":"\^[[31mab\^[[0m","firstWidth":2,"second":"pla","secondWidth":3,"third":"\^[[32mx\^[[0m","thirdWidth":1}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The first and third styled truncations kept their ANSI wrappers while reducing visible width to 2 and 1, and the intervening plain truncation returned `pla` with visible width 3, showing the shared scanner did not leak state between calls.
- What was not tested: broad OpenClaw suite / Crabbox.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: change is isolated to `packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.ts`; validation used focused terminal-core runtime and unit coverage rather than broad end-to-end packaging.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): not captured; the change preserves behavior and removes repeated regex construction rather than fixing a visible output failure.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.ts packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.test.ts` (7 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.test.ts packages/terminal-core/src/table.test.ts` (29 passed)
- `node --import tsx -e "import { truncateToVisibleWidth, visibleWidth } from './packages/terminal-core/src/ansi.ts'; const first = truncateToVisibleWidth('\\x1b[31mabc\\x1b[0m', 2); const second = truncateToVisibleWidth('plain', 3); const third = truncateToVisibleWidth('\\x1b[32mxy\\x1b[0m', 1); console.log(JSON.stringify({ first, firstWidth: visibleWidth(first), second, secondWidth: visibleWidth(second), third, thirdWidth: visibleWidth(third) }));"`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `codex review --uncommitted` (no actionable regressions)
- `git fetch origin main; codex review --base origin/main` (no actionable correctness issues)

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added a focused regression test for repeated styled/plain `truncateToVisibleWidth` calls to ensure the shared scanner does not leak regex state across calls.

What failed before this fix, if known?
- No visible behavior failure; this is a small performance cleanup for repeated truncation calls.

If no test was added, why not?
- A regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
- No.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
- No.

What is the highest-risk area?
- Reusing a global regex with the `g` flag could carry `lastIndex` state if not reset.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The helper resets `ANSI_SEQUENCE_REGEX.lastIndex = 0` before every scan, and the new test exercises repeated styled/plain calls.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review / CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- Waiting on GitHub CI and maintainer review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- None yet.

target.title: fix(runtime): snapshot tool definitions
target.body:
## Summary
- Snapshot plugin command registrations through guarded field reads before validation and storage.
- Snapshot package-level `Agent` state tools and `prepareNextTurn` replacement contexts before provider exposure, sharing the same quarantine helper with the harness path.
- Snapshot direct `agentLoop`/`agentLoopContinue` contexts and `prepareNextTurn` replacement contexts before provider exposure, covering low-level runtime callers below `Agent`/`CoreAgentHarness`.
- Guard embedded-agent runtime tool-name collection for prompt rendering, session allowlists, bundle reserved names, context metadata, and diagnostics so unreadable tool/client descriptors cannot crash pre-provider setup.
- Guard runtime-plan/provider-normalized tool metadata copying so cloned normalized tools with unreadable names cannot crash before later quarantine and still keep same-index plugin provenance.
- Make provider and RuntimePlan tool-schema diagnostic hooks best-effort so a broken schema inspector cannot abort the assistant turn before content.
- Guard embedded-attempt explicit allowlist filtering so unreadable bundle/plugin tool names or throwing plugin metadata lookups cannot abort tool construction before quarantine.
- Guard shared tool-policy metadata and name reads so inherited allowlists, plugin-group expansion, policy filtering, and audit logs fail closed without pre-content crashes.
- Snapshot package-level `CoreAgentHarness` tool registries before constructor/setter exposure so malformed SDK/runtime harness tools are skipped without killing healthy active tools.
- Snapshot core session tool definitions before embedded-agent/session registration, so malformed runtime tool descriptors are skipped without killing healthy sibling tools.
- Snapshot session extension registrations, SDK custom tools, base-tool overrides, and `ToolDefinition`/`AgentTool` wrappers before session startup so malformed registered definitions are quarantined per tool.
- Snapshot Copilot bridged OpenClaw tools before SDK registration, bridge filtering, and duplicate detection so malformed bridged tool metadata is quarantined per tool.
- Snapshot realtime voice tools before provider exposure in shared Talk merging plus OpenAI and Google realtime session construction, so one malformed custom tool/schema is skipped while built-in and healthy tools survive.
- Snapshot Mistral, Anthropic provider, native Anthropic transport, OpenAI-compatible completions, OpenAI transport-owned Responses/Chat Completions, native Ollama, Amazon Bedrock, Google prompt-cache, and Google transport request tools before SDK/provider payload construction, skip unreadable/proxy-backed schemas for normal requests, and fail closed when required provider `toolChoice` semantics cannot be honored after quarantine.
- Harden the shared plain-text tool-call compatibility wrapper so unreadable tool names do not disable healthy provider stream repair.
- Snapshot manifest-backed plugin tool schemas during live resolution and cached descriptor execution so volatile or proxy-backed plugin tool metadata cannot poison healthy sibling tools.
- Guard implicit plugin tool registration names so a poisoned object-tool `name` getter cannot abort later healthy registrations from the same plugin.
- Snapshot provider-family tool schema helpers for Gemini/OpenAI/DeepSeek compatibility so unreadable provider plugin tool metadata is quarantined per tool while normalized tools keep enumerable SDK fields.
- Snapshot MCP plugin tool bridge metadata before before-call wrapping so unreadable plugin tool fields are quarantined per tool, poisoned execute bind accessors cannot abort the bridge, and plugin ownership metadata stays attached.
- Project Codex dynamic tool schemas through a guarded shared helper so unreadable runtime tool metadata is quarantined before bridge construction while healthy tools still reach Codex.
- Snapshot Codex dynamic tool names/descriptions/projected schemas before bridge wrapping so volatile descriptors cannot crash map/spec/telemetry construction after projection.
- Snapshot shared OpenAI Responses tools before strict-mode conversion so unreadable or hostile schemas skip per tool without downgrading healthy strict-compatible tools.
- Preserve plugin ownership in doctor and effective tool inventory quarantine warnings when pre-normalization diagnostics come from tools with unreadable names.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: plugin command registration now handles unreadable command metadata as a failed registration and keeps later healthy commands registerable; stored optional metadata is copied before plugin-owned objects can mutate. Package-level `Agent`, direct `agentLoop`/`agentLoopContinue`, and `CoreAgentHarness` tool registries now snapshot state, constructor, setter, prompt-run, continue-run, context-snapshot, and `prepareNextTurn` replacement tools before turn-context exposure, so unreadable package SDK/runtime tool names or parameter schemas are skipped while healthy active tools remain available and schema payloads are cloned. Embedded-agent runtime name collection now guards session allowlists, bundle MCP/LSP reserved-name sets, bootstrap file-access detection, context-engine available-tool metadata, embedded prompt tool guidance, and provider schema diagnostic formatting, so unreadable tool `name` getters or client tool `function` getters cannot crash pre-provider setup or warning logs. Runtime-plan/provider normalization metadata copying now guards cloned normalized tool names and keeps same-index plugin provenance when a normalized target name becomes unreadable, so provider cleanup cannot crash before later quarantine. Provider and RuntimePlan tool-schema diagnostic hooks now warn and return when schema inspection throws, so best-effort diagnostics cannot abort the assistant turn before content. Embedded-attempt explicit allowlist filtering now captures readable names before policy matching and skips unreadable names, so bad bundle/plugin tools cannot crash allowlist filtering before runtime projection; throwing plugin metadata lookups are treated as missing metadata while exact healthy tool-name matches still work. Shared tool-policy paths now guard runtime tool name reads for inherited allowlist replacement, plugin-group expansion, core/plugin classification, configured filtering, and audit logging; tools whose policy-critical plugin metadata cannot be read are dropped fail-closed before plugin-id/group policy expansion. Core embedded-agent/session tool definitions now snapshot runtime tool metadata before registration, so unreadable runtime tool names or parameters are quarantined per tool while healthy session definitions remain available. Session extension `api.registerTool`, SDK custom tools, `AgentSession` base-tool overrides, and `ToolDefinition`/`AgentTool` wrappers now snapshot registered definitions before session startup, preserve TypeBox metadata, keep top-level `undefined` schemas for no-argument/client tools, and skip malformed registered definitions without dropping healthy tools. Copilot bridge now snapshots bridged OpenClaw tools before SDK registration, allowlist filtering, duplicate detection, and direct conversion, so unreadable names or nested schemas are quarantined per tool while healthy bridged tools remain available. Custom realtime voice tools are snapshotted before provider exposure in shared Talk merging and in OpenAI and Google native/browser realtime sessions, so unreadable custom tool metadata or nested schema getters do not abort the realtime tool list, Live connect config, client-secret request body, or browser token constraints. Mistral, Anthropic provider, native Anthropic transport, OpenAI-compatible completions, OpenAI transport-owned Responses/Chat Completions, native Ollama, Amazon Bedrock, Google prompt-cache, and Google transport request tools are snapshotted before SDK/provider payload construction, so revoked descriptors, proxy schemas, and nested schema getters are skipped for normal requests while healthy tools remain available; explicit required provider `toolChoice` settings fail closed when they point at quarantined or unavailable tools. The shared plain-text tool-call compatibility wrapper now ignores unreadable tool names while preserving healthy repair names. Manifest-backed plugin tool resolution now snapshots readable tool name/schema/execute metadata once, stabilizes live tool wrappers without violating non-configurable property invariants, preserves class-backed metadata/callback scoping, drops descriptor-trap tools fail-closed, and cached descriptor execution reuses cached schemas instead of rereading volatile runtime schemas. Plugin SDK `api.registerTool(objectTool)` now treats an unreadable implicit object-tool name as a per-registration diagnostic, so later healthy tool registrations from the same plugin still load; explicit registration names are used without rereading the object tool name. provider-family schema helpers now quarantine unreadable provider plugin tool names/parameters during Gemini, OpenAI, and DeepSeek compatibility passes while preserving normalized tool enumerable fields. MCP plugin tool bridge construction now skips unreadable plugin-owned tool metadata per tool, avoids execute.bind reads, and preserves plugin ownership metadata on the wrapped snapshot. Codex dynamic tool bridge construction now quarantines unreadable runtime tool schemas before reading raw tool metadata, snapshots projected names/descriptions/schemas before bridge wrapping, and keeps healthy dynamic tools available. Shared OpenAI Responses tool conversion now skips unreadable or hostile tool schemas per tool while keeping healthy strict-compatible tools strict. Doctor and effective tool inventory quarantine warnings now keep plugin attribution for pre-normalization diagnostics even when the bad tool name is unreadable.

target.title: fix(matrix): suppress noisy cron tool-timeout alerts
target.body:
## Summary
- suppress user-facing cron failure notifications for the exact isolated-runner watchdog timeout `cron: job execution timed out (last phase: tool-execution-started)`
- preserve the cron run as an error with `lastError` intact
- mark suppressed failure-notification delivery as `not-requested` instead of leaving it ambiguous
- keep ordinary cron failures, generic timeouts, webhook destinations, and provider/model failures notifying normally

## Proof

### Redacted live behavior that motivated this fix

A private Matrix-facing workflow produced a scary user-visible failure notice even though later investigation showed the underlying cron path recovered. The private room, sender, Matrix event IDs, cron job ID, exact room label, and surrounding transcript are intentionally omitted.

This PR targets one narrow false-alert class observed during investigation: isolated cron runner watchdog timeouts where the embedded agent had already reached tool execution:

`cron: job execution timed out (last phase: tool-execution-started)`

The intended behavior is not to erase the cron error. The cron error must remain recorded in state. The only suppressed behavior is the user-facing failure notification for this narrow timeout class.

### Non-PII after-fix behavior proof

The proof uses synthetic cron jobs and the synthetic timeout reason above.

Gateway behavior proof:
- `src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts` verifies that a synthetic `tool-execution-started` watchdog timeout does not send the primary user-facing announce notification.
- The same file verifies that a synthetic `tool-execution-started` watchdog timeout does not send a failure-destination notification.

Cron state proof:
- `src/cron/service.persists-delivered-status.test.ts` verifies the timeout remains recorded as a cron error.
- It verifies failure notification delivery is marked `not-requested`.
- It covers delivered and not-delivered result cases so the suppression does not depend on ambiguous delivery state.

Commands run:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts -t "does not announce tool-execution-started cron watchdog timeouts|does not send failure-destination notifications for tool-execution-started cron watchdog timeouts"` — 2 passed, 18 skipped
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts src/cron/service.persists-delivered-status.test.ts -t "keeps tool-execution-started timeouts recorded without requesting failure notification delivery|keeps primary announce tool-execution-started timeouts from expecting fallback failure notifications|suppresses failure notification state for delivered tool-execution-started timeouts|suppresses failure notification state for not-delivered tool-execution-started timeouts"` — 4 passed, 12 skipped

Previously run full branch checks:
- Gateway cron tests — 20 passed
- Cron delivery-status tests — 16 passed
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm -s lint:core`
- `pnpm -s tsgo:core:test`

### Scope note

This PR does not hide real cron failures generally. Generic timeouts, provider/model failures, webhook destinations, and ordinary cron failures still notify normally. It only suppresses the narrow false-alert notification path while keeping the cron error visible in run state.

### Redaction note

No live Matrix room IDs, user IDs, message IDs, cron IDs, room labels, private transcript text, or private job contents are included. The proof uses synthetic cron inputs and the minimum public-safe timeout string needed to demonstrate the behavior.

target.title: fix: add timeout to waitForWaConnection to prevent indefinite hangs
target.body:
## Problem

`waitForWaConnection` listens for a Baileys `connection.update` event with either `'open'` or `'close'` status, but has no timeout. If Baileys fails to emit either event (e.g. due to network issues, DNS resolution failures, or internal errors), the promise hangs indefinitely, blocking the entire WhatsApp monitor loop from reconnecting.

## Fix

Add a configurable `timeoutMs` parameter (default 60s) that:
- Rejects the promise with a descriptive error if no connection state is received in time
- Cleans up the event listener on timeout (prevents listener leaks)
- Cleans up the timer on normal resolution (prevents dangling timers)

The change is backward-compatible — the timeout parameter is optional with a sensible default. Callers that pass `0` get the old behavior (wait forever).

## Testing

- All existing tests pass (the function is mocked in tests via `vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(undefined)`)
- The new timeout path follows the same cleanup pattern as the existing `close` path

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
Fixes #90013

## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

### Layer 1: Download failure guards

Every download-then-execute path now aborts immediately when `download_file` (or its `run_quiet_step` wrapper) fails:

- `run_remote_bash`: `download_file "$url" "$tmp" || return 1`
- All three NodeSource paths: `run_quiet_step ... download_file ... || return 1`

Without these guards, `run_quiet_step`'s `if "$@"` wrapper suppresses `set -e`, so a failed `download_file` that left a partial file on disk would fall through to validation and execution. This addresses the [P2] finding from ClawSweeper review.

### Layer 2: Structural validation

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`) using raw byte check (`od -An -tx1 -N2`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F, 0x7F, 0x80-0x9F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d` and escapes backslashes so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` and three NodeSource paths executed downloaded content unconditionally. Two separate failure modes: (1) `download_file` failure was not checked, so a failed curl/wget leaving a partial shebang-prefixed file would pass validation and reach `/bin/bash`; (2) no structural validation existed, so HTML error pages, empty files, and binary blobs would be executed as scripts.

Real environment tested: macOS (bash 3.2). The test harness sources the production `scripts/install.sh` with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1` and calls the real `validate_downloaded_script` function directly, proving the actual production code path works.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
```

Evidence after fix (commit 6691553f):
```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh

-- GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads --
  PASS  empty file  -> rejected
  PASS  HTML error   -> rejected
  PASS  JSON error   -> rejected
  PASS  binary blob  -> rejected
  PASS  C1 escape    -> rejected
  PASS  NUL prefix   -> rejected (raw byte check caught NUL before #!)
  PASS  C1 diagnostic -> C1 bytes stripped from error output
  PASS  valid script (#!bash)     -> accepted
  PASS  valid script (#!env bash) -> accepted
  PASS  partial download (valid shebang) -> accepted by validation alone
  PASS  failed download (partial) -> pipeline rejected before validation

-- RED: no validation lets bad downloads through --
  PASS  empty file  -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  HTML error   -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  JSON error   -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  binary blob  -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 15 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed
```

Key design points:
- The harness sources the production `scripts/install.sh` with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1` and calls `validate_downloaded_script` directly (not a copied duplicate)
- Binary fixture uses deterministic non-shebang bytes (PNG magic `\x89PNG`) instead of `/dev/urandom`, eliminating the theoretical false-accept when random bytes happen to be `#!`
- Tests 10-11 prove the download_file failure guard: `partial.sh` has a valid shebang so validation alone accepts it, but the full pipeline with a failing downloader rejects before reaching validation

Observed result after fix: All 15 harness tests pass. Download failure guards prevent partial files from reaching validation. Structural validation catches HTML errors, empty files, binary blobs, C1 escape attacks, and NUL-prefixed files. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against terminal injection.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local fixtures instead).

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011
Fixes #82304

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Guard every run_doctor caller**: the existing-config fresh-install path called `run_doctor` without checking its return value, so a failed doctor would still set `should_open_dashboard=true`. Now guarded with `if run_doctor; then should_open_dashboard=true; fi`
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** (1) Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. (2) An unguarded `run_doctor` call site could set the dashboard flag even on doctor failure.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw main at commit 9788648abd (rebased).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Sent SIGINT to the installer during the npm install phase of a real upgrade on macOS:
  ```bash
  set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  ```

  Step 2. Verified every `run_doctor` caller is guarded:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'run_doctor' scripts/install.sh
  ```
  All call sites use `if run_doctor; then` pattern, no bare `run_doctor` calls outside the function definition.

  Step 3. Ran focused tests covering doctor cancellation and dashboard guard:
  ```bash
  npx vitest run test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts --reporter verbose 2>&1 | grep -E "doctor|cancel|dashboard|guard"
  ```
  ```console
  ✓ guards every run_doctor caller against failure
  ✓ clears dashboard flag when doctor fails during upgrade
  ✓ propagates signal exit codes through run_quiet_step
  ✓ aborts on SIGINT (exit 130) from doctor
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the patched install script:

  SIGINT during the install phase triggers clean abort with exit 130:

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"

  Preparing installer interface...

    OpenClaw Installer

  Detected: macos
  Existing OpenClaw installation detected, upgrading

  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js v24.15.0 found
  Using Node.js runtime at /Users/user/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  Git already installed
  Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.28
  Installing OpenClaw package

  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  All 48 install.sh tests pass (1 pre-existing unrelated `min-release-age` failure excluded). The focused tests verify the `run_doctor` guard at every call site via regex scanning.

target.title: fix(edit): preserve unrelated lines during fuzzy text matching
target.body:
Fixes #89994

## Summary

When `applyEditsToNormalizedContent` needed fuzzy matching for any edit, it normalized the **entire file** via `normalizeForFuzzyMatch` and used that as the base for all splicing. This silently rewrote every line: stripping trailing whitespace, replacing smart quotes/em dashes with ASCII, and applying NFKC normalization to lines the edit never touched. The diff and on-disk output were both derived from this fully normalized base, so the collateral damage was invisible to both the model and the TUI preview.

## Fix

Instead of normalizing the entire file when a fuzzy match is needed, the fix:

1. Keeps the original content as `baseContent` (always)
2. Adds a `mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal` helper that maps character positions from fuzzy-normalized space back to original space using a line-based offset table
3. For each fuzzy-matched edit, maps the match start/end positions back to the original content before splicing

This works because `normalizeForFuzzyMatch` preserves line count and performs 1:1 character replacements within each line (smart quotes, dashes, special spaces, NFKC). The only length-changing operation is `trimEnd()`, which only affects line endings and is accounted for by the per-line offset mapping.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A single fuzzy edit silently normalizes and rewrites every line in the file, corrupting smart quotes, em dashes, and trailing whitespace on lines the edit never touched.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-89994`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify fix in compiled dist:

```bash
cd /tmp/wt-89994
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build
grep -n "mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal" dist/sessions-Vxbh9RFF.js
```

Step 2. Run tsx proof script that exercises the production code path:

```bash
npx tsx proof-89994.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the patched build:

The fix is active in the production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal" dist/sessions-Vxbh9RFF.js
5204:function mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal(origLines, normLines, normalizedOffset) {
5245:const origStart = mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal(origLines, normLines, matchResult.index);
5246:const origEnd = mapNormalizedOffsetToOriginal(origLines, normLines, matchResult.index + matchResult.matchLength);
```

Terminal confirms fuzzy edits now preserve unrelated lines:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-89994.ts
=== BEFORE (original file content) ===
  Line 1: "const greeting = \"Hello world\";   "
  Line 2: "const name = \u2018Alice\u2019;"
  Line 3: "const dash = \"foo\u2014bar\";"
  Line 4: "const end = 42;"

=== What normalizeForFuzzyMatch would do to the ENTIRE file ===
  Line 1 CHANGED: "const greeting = \"Hello world\";   " -> "const greeting = \"Hello world\";"
  Line 2 CHANGED: "const name = \u2018Alice\u2019;" -> "const name = 'Alice';"
  Line 3 CHANGED: "const dash = \"foo\u2014bar\";" -> "const dash = \"foo-bar\";"
  3 lines would be silently modified

=== Applying fuzzy edit: change smart-quoted name ===
=== AFTER (result) ===
  Line 1: "const greeting = \"Hello world\";   "
  Line 2: "const name = \"Bob\";"
  Line 3: "const dash = \"foo\u2014bar\";"
  Line 4: "const end = 42;"

=== Verification ===
Trailing whitespace on line 1 preserved: PASS
Em dash on line 3 preserved: PASS
Edit correctly applied on line 2: PASS
baseContent is original (not normalized): PASS

ALL CHECKS PASSED: fuzzy edit only affects the matched region
```

Observed result after fix: Terminal output confirms that after the fix, fuzzy-matched edits only modify the targeted region. Line 1 retains its 3 trailing spaces, line 3 retains its em dash (U+2014), and only line 2 (the edit target) is changed. Before this fix, all 3 lines would have been silently normalized.

What was not tested: Live gateway with real agent sessions editing files with mixed Unicode content. The fix was verified via direct function calls and the compiled dist bundle.

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends PR #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` via the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver). Same dual-key gating shape as #85664 — distinct surface-specific opt-in flag PLUS per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` — so existing configs that already list `"write"` or `"edit"` in `allow` for unrelated surfaces (e.g. an MCP/agent surface) stay inert at the direct-invoke layer.

**This PR is the redesigned narrow write extension.** The prior 5-commit history was reset to a single fresh commit on top of #85664's `d9315f358c` — no rebase conflicts, just a clean extension that satisfies ClawSweeper's [P1] from the prior review.

## Dual-key gating (mirrors #85664's pattern)

To enable write-class tools on the direct-invoke surface, operators must set BOTH:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | **NEW.** Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. Without this, no write tool is materialized into the candidate set even if `tools.allow` includes its name. | `false` (omitted) |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` | Per-tool names the operator wants enabled. Subset OK — e.g. `["edit"]` alone enables only `edit`. | not present |

**Either key alone leaves write tools unreachable.** Old configs with `allow: ["write"]` (for an unrelated surface) stay inert.

Truth table (per tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`):

| `tools.allow` includes `T` | `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` | `T` reachable on direct-invoke |
|---|---|---|
| no  | no   | ❌ |
| yes | no   | ❌ (tool not materialized) |
| no  | yes  | ❌ (filtered by HTTP deny) |
| yes | yes  | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (via `hostFsWrite: true` opt-in).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` opt-in (unchanged regression test).
- **`apply_patch`:** added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for future-proofing, but NOT currently produced by the coding tool factory — deferred until upstream adds a factory entry.
- **NOT exposed (deferred):** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell`. These are RCE-class and require a separate owner/admin enforcement model — `operator.write` scope alone is not sufficient. Distinct follow-up PR.

## Files changed (delta against #85664)

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` (apply_patch already present upstream)
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — extends the existing read materialization block to also include write/edit when `hostFsWrite=true`
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` finding (only fires when both gates set + at least one write tool name in allow; silent otherwise)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — 6 new test cases covering all gate combinations + cross-class isolation (hostFsWrite + only read in allow → silent)
- `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` — operator-facing dual-key opt-in docs + truth table + explicit note about deferred exec/process/spawn/shell

## Real-behavior proof on head `d9315f358c`

**Build:** `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container, `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs`. Output: `dist/tool-resolution-Blc5CWzR.js` (contains `hostFsWrite`) and `dist/audit-DHrX1aSk.js` (contains `host_write_allow`).

**Driver:** Node ESM script imports `resolveGatewayScopedTools` from the freshly-compiled bundle, calls it with 10 config shapes covering: read regression (4 cases mirroring #85664), write dual-key gating (4 cases), mixed read+write (1 case), and an RCE-attempt case proving exec/process/spawn/shell never materialize even with all gates set.

```
[bundle] /work/dist/tool-resolution-Blc5CWzR.js
[resolve] resolveGatewayScopedTools found

[R-VANILLA]                                read=  write=  total=23  ✅
[R-ALLOW-ONLY  allow=["read"]]             read=  write=  total=23  ✅
[R-FLAG-ONLY   hostFsRead=true]            read=  write=  total=23  ✅
[R-BOTH        allow+flag]                 read=✓ write=  total=24  ✅
[W-ALLOW-ONLY  allow=write,edit,apply_p]   read=  write=  total=23  ✅
[W-FLAG-ONLY   hostFsWrite=true]           read=  write=  total=23  ✅
[W-BOTH-FULL   allow+flag]                 read=  write=✓ edit=✓    ✅
[W-BOTH-SUBSET allow=[edit] + flag]        read=  write=  edit=✓    ✅
[M-FULL        read+write allow+both flags] read=✓ write=✓ edit=    ✅
[RCE-ATTEMPT   allow=exec,process,shell,spawn + both flags]
                                            exec= process= shell= spawn=  ✅

=== ALL PASS === (10/10)
```

**Key results:**

- **W-BOTH-FULL** ✅ — both keys set + all write names in allow → `write` and `edit` are reachable. (`apply_patch` correctly absent — factory doesn't produce it yet, deferred for future.)
- **W-BOTH-SUBSET** ✅ — `allow: ["edit"]` + `hostFsWrite: true` → only `edit` reachable; `write` correctly absent because not in allow. Per-tool granularity verified.
- **W-ALLOW-ONLY** ✅ — bot-P1 critical case: legacy config with `allow: ["write", "edit", "apply_patch"]` alone does NOT grant write access on direct-invoke. The new `hostFsWrite: true` opt-in is required additionally.
- **RCE-ATTEMPT** ✅ — even with `allow: ["exec", "process", "shell", "spawn"]` AND both `hostFsRead` + `hostFsWrite` set, the RCE-class tools NEVER appear in the candidate set. This is structural (the materialization block only includes coding tool names, never RCE tools), not policy-filtered.

## Code-level test coverage

`src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` (new `describe` block for host_write_allow):

- ✅ fires (warn) when both keys set + `allow: ["write"]` on loopback
- ✅ fires for `allow: ["edit", "apply_patch"]` + `hostFsWrite: true`
- ✅ escalates to critical when bind is lan
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `allow: ["write"]` (legacy shape)
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `hostFsWrite: true`
- ✅ does NOT fire when `hostFsWrite: true` + `allow: ["read"]` (cross-class — different opt-in needed)

## Why a single fresh commit (not rebase of prior 5)

The original PR's 5 commits implemented a single-key `allow: ["exec", "process"]` opt-in with an `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag that the bot correctly flagged as DEAD CODE after upstream commit `02182d5a3031` removed the owner-only enforcement helper. Patching on top of those 5 commits to fix the bot's [P1] would have layered design-on-design. Cleaner to reset to #85664's head and write the redesign as one commit.

Backup of prior PR history retained at `backup/63919-pre-rebase-20260603-onto-85664` on the public fork.

## Why exec/process/spawn/shell are deferred

The bot's prior [P1] on this PR ("Enforce the exec/process owner gate") flagged that with upstream's removal of sender-owner gating, an `ownerOnly: true` tag is metadata-only — a trusted-proxy caller with `operator.write` can still reach allowlisted `exec`/`process` without owner check.

A `directInvoke.commandExec: true` flag (analogous to `hostFsWrite`) is **not sufficient** for the RCE-class because:
- `operator.write` is broadly distributed in trusted-proxy setups
- RCE-class needs `operator.admin` or `senderIsOwner` enforcement at the direct-invoke layer
- This requires threading principal context through `invokeGatewayTool` in `tools-invoke-shared.ts` — a separate refactor

That refactor belongs in its own PR with its own review surface. This PR ships the safe write extension and explicitly documents the deferral.

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Extend #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over Gateway direct-invoke surfaces, using the same dual-key gating pattern (`gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` plus per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow`). Addresses ClawSweeper [P1] on the prior PR head (the dead `ownerOnly` tag); RCE-class tools (exec/process/spawn/shell) remain unavailable and are deferred to a follow-up PR.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `git checkout d9315f358c && docker run --rm -v $PWD:/work -w /work --platform linux/arm64 --memory=12g node:22-bookworm-slim bash -c 'set -e; corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@11.2.2 --activate && node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs && node /work/.proof-driver-63919.mjs'` — exits 0 with `=== ALL PASS ===` (10/10).

target.title: feat(approvals): add plain-language plugin approvals
target.body:
## Mission

Plugin approvals are a security boundary, but they are often delivered in chat where the old fallback text read like internal debug output. A normal approval could show a raw title, full command text, proposed exec policy, session key, tool/plugin IDs, expiry, and `/approve <id>` syntax all at once.

That raw format is useful for maintainers, but it is not easy for a Telegram/Matrix user to scan quickly. The goal of this PR is to make opt-in plugin approvals understandable at a glance while preserving the legacy raw behavior by default.

In short: keep the original technical approval prompt as the default, and add a plain-language approval mode for users who explicitly choose it.

## What This Adds

This PR adds `approvals.plugin.language` with three modes:

- `original`: existing raw technical plugin approval text. This remains the default.
- `simple`: a short plain-language prompt with `Action`, `It will`, `Risk`, and available choices.
- `simple-technical`: the plain-language prompt plus the raw technical fields below it.

Example config:

```json
{
  "approvals": {
    "plugin": {
      "language": "simple"
    }
  }
}
```

The new renderer keeps interactive approval metadata intact so native Telegram/Matrix approval buttons can still be rendered. Text-only channels still get an actionable `/approve plugin:<id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` fallback.

## Implementation

- Added the typed `PluginApprovalLanguage` config surface and validation.
- Wired the configured language through shared plugin approval rendering.
- Forwarded the language option through fallback delivery and native Telegram/Matrix approval payloads.
- Added plain-English approval output with risk text and decision-aware fallback instructions.
- Added `Command preview` output for commands that are high risk or cannot be summarized completely.
- Redacted token/password/API-key/bearer/basic-auth/cookie/userinfo forms from previews before they can be posted into chat.
- Updated generated config and plugin SDK API baselines.
- Added focused renderer and forwarder tests.

## Safety Model

The important rule is: if `simple` mode hides raw technical details, the summary must either explain the meaningful action or fail closed.

Fail closed means the approval becomes higher risk and shows a redacted command preview instead of pretending the command is harmless.

The renderer now handles and tests common Codex app-server command shapes:

- shell wrappers: `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, `zsh -c`, `env`, `timeout`, `set -a`, `source`;
- shell control: `&&`, `||`, `;`, background `&`, conditionals, loops, and attached redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`;
- pipelines: known stages are summarized stage-by-stage; unknown stages become high risk with a preview;
- file reads/writes/deletes: `cat`, `sed`, `find`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, redirection writes;
- network transfers: `curl` and `wget` URL targets, upload operands, output operands, stdin upload via redirection, config/header/cookie/auth/netrc forms;
- credentials: bearer headers, OAuth bearer flags, basic auth, proxy auth, cookie headers, netrc credential sources, URL userinfo;
- dev/ops commands: `git`, `docker`, service managers, package managers, interpreters, logs/process inspection;
- remote commands: `ssh`, `scp`, SSH remote commands, and SSH local-execution options such as `ProxyCommand` and `LocalCommand`.

## Review And Hardening Trail

This PR went through a long approval-safety review loop because turning verbose technical prompts into plain language makes the summarizer security-sensitive.

Issues fixed during review:

- Preserved actionable text fallback instructions for channels without buttons.
- Made pipelines visible before raw command text can be hidden.
- Made command target extraction command-aware so boolean flags like `cat -n .env` do not hide sensitive targets.
- Surfaced curl/wget upload and output operands, including `.env` style local-file transfers.
- Handled stdin uploads such as `curl --data-binary @- ... < .env`.
- Redacted basic-auth, OAuth bearer, cookie, header, and URL credential forms from command previews.
- Failed closed on curl config files and curl header-file inputs.
- Surfaced wget credential/header/cookie options.
- Showed targets for redirected formatter writes like `printf ... > .env`.
- Handled background shell separators such as `sleep 1 & rm -rf /tmp/x`.
- Parsed Git global options such as `git -C repo reset --hard` before classifying subcommands.
- Treated `find` output-file predicates like `-fprint`, `-fprint0`, `-fls`, and `-fprintf` as writes.
- Detected attached shell redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`.
- Failed closed on SSH local-execution options such as `ssh -o ProxyCommand=...` and `ssh -o LocalCommand=...`.
- Refreshed against latest `upstream/main` repeatedly and regenerated the config baseline after conflicts.
- Stabilized cron test mock state exposed by the refreshed CI shard.

The latest ClawSweeper review no longer reports a concrete repair item. It now asks for human maintainer review of the opt-in approval-safety boundary and branch scope.

## Current Status

- Latest head at time of this update: `91d6303a0e`.
- GitHub reports the PR mergeable and clean.
- CI/checks are green on the latest head.
- `Real behavior proof` is successful.
- ClawSweeper durable review status: needs maintainer review before merge, not an automated repair.
- Remaining decision: maintainers need to accept the opt-in plain-language approval UX/security boundary, or ask for scope splitting if they want the unrelated refreshed-base/test-stability churn separated.

## Verification

Local verification after the latest SSH hardening and main refresh:

- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`

Earlier focused verification also covered config schema/help quality, plugin SDK API baseline, gateway/channel state, and live renderer output.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Chat-delivered plugin approvals can now render as a short plain-English approval card instead of a raw technical dump when `approvals.plugin.language` is set to `simple`. The old raw format remains the default through `original`.
- Real environment tested: Hadi's live OpenClaw source checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 (`/home/hadi/src/openclaw`), the PR worktree, and Telegram channel status in polling mode.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rendered real approval messages through `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage(..., { language: "simple" })`.
  - Ran focused approval renderer and forwarder tests.
  - Ran type, lint, config docs, and baseline checks.
  - Cherry-picked the feature into the live source checkout during testing, ran `pnpm build`, restarted `openclaw-gateway.service`, and checked gateway/Telegram channel state.
- Evidence after fix: terminal renderer output showed the intended card shape:

```text
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Create/check workspace files or folders

It will
- create folder(s): memory
- check whether a condition or file exists: memory/context.md

Risk: Low
Only creates or checks workspace files. No network, service, or delete step detected.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:example allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Pipeline and unknown-stage proof:

```text
--- pipeline curl ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Use the network or download data

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- make a network request or download data: https://example.test/upload

Risk: Medium
Network commands can send or fetch data outside this machine.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:pipeline allow-once|allow-always|deny

--- unknown pipeline ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Run a terminal command

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- run custom-uploader

Command preview
cat notes.txt | custom-uploader --send

Risk: High
This command is part of a pipeline I cannot fully summarize, so review it before approving.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:unknown allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Live channel state checked during proof:

```text
Telegram default (Telegram): enabled, configured, running, connected, mode:polling, token:tokenFile
```

Observed result after fix:

- `simple` mode no longer includes the raw technical details block.
- Escaped shell separators such as `&&` are decoded before parsing.
- Shell conditionals no longer surface as fake `run if` / `run then` actions.
- Non-shell pipeline stages are visible or fail closed with a high-risk preview.
- Sensitive command previews are redacted before being shown in chat.
- SSH local-execution options now fail closed with a high-risk preview.

- What was not tested:

- The Mantis Telegram Desktop GIF run skipped instead of producing before/after GIFs. This PR still has terminal renderer proof, live gateway/channel state proof, earlier Telegram screenshot proof, and a successful `Real behavior proof` check.

target.title: fix(agents): keep apostrophes in slash-command prompt-template arguments
target.body:
## Summary

- `parseCommandArgs` (`packages/agent-core/src/harness/prompt-template-arguments.ts`) expands `$ARGUMENTS` / `$@` / `$1..$N` in user prompt templates and custom slash commands. It does shell-style quote parsing with **no escape and no unbalanced-quote recovery**, so a lone `'` — an ordinary apostrophe in English prose (`user's`, `it's`, `don't`) — opens a quote that runs to the next apostrophe (or end of string). The apostrophe is dropped and the following whitespace is swallowed into one token. The corrupted tokens then feed `$ARGUMENTS` (`args.join(" ")`) and the positional placeholders, so the model **silently receives a mangled instruction** with no error or warning.
  - `/cmd don't it's broken now` + a `[$1][$2]` template collapses to `ARG1=[dont its] ARG2=[broken]` — both apostrophes dropped, placeholders shifted.
- Reach: shipped and default-on. `AgentSession.prompt()` expands templates by default (`expandPromptTemplates ?? true`) and the **expanded text becomes the user message sent to the model**. Templates are real user `.md` files loaded by `loadPromptTemplates` from `<agentDir>/prompts`, `<cwd>/.openclaw/prompts`, and explicit prompt paths.
- Fix: an apostrophe that sits **inside a word** (a word character on both sides — a contraction like `don't`, `it's`, `José's`) is treated as a literal and can neither open nor close a quoted span. Every other quote still behaves as a shell-style delimiter, so balanced spans (`alpha "beta gamma"`) and mid-token spans (`--title="two words"`, `foo='bar baz'`) group exactly as before. Word-character detection is Unicode-aware (`\p{L}`/`\p{N}`), so non-ASCII prose like `José's` is preserved, and an unmatched leading quote (`'review don't fail`) no longer latches onto a later contraction.
- One canonical change in `parseCommandArgs`; both consumers (`expandPromptTemplate` in `src/agents/sessions/prompt-templates.ts` and `formatPromptTemplateInvocation` in `packages/agent-core`) benefit with no API change. The separate `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.ts` `parseCommandArgs` (native channel commands) is a different function and is left untouched.

## Tests

- `packages/agent-core/src/harness/prompt-templates.test.ts`: added cases covering apostrophes preserved in parsed tokens and through `$ARGUMENTS` / positional placeholders; multiple contractions in one input; shell-style quoted spans embedded in a token (`--title="two words"`, `foo='bar baz'`); apostrophes after non-ASCII word characters (`José's don't fail`); and an unmatched leading quote that must not close on a later contraction (`'review don't fail`). The existing quoted/multiline and placeholder-safety cases are unchanged and green.
- `src/agents/sessions/prompt-templates.test.ts` (`expandPromptTemplate`) unchanged and green.
- Touched suites run locally with the repo runner; type-aware oxlint + oxfmt clean on the touched files.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a user-authored custom slash command / prompt template invoked with ordinary prose containing an apostrophe silently lost the apostrophe (and, for positional `$N` templates, collapsed arguments and shifted later placeholders), so the model received a corrupted instruction with no error or warning. Covers ASCII contractions, multiple contractions in one input, non-ASCII contractions, and an unmatched leading quote.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22.19, exercising the actual production functions `loadPromptTemplates` + `expandPromptTemplate` — the same expansion path `AgentSession.prompt()` uses to build the outbound user message — over a real on-disk prompt directory (`<agentDir>/prompts/*.md`).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote `<tmp>/prompts/review.md` = `Please address: $ARGUMENTS` and `<tmp>/prompts/cmd.md` = `ARG1=[$1] ARG2=[$2] ARG3=[$3] ARG4=[$4]`; loaded them via `loadPromptTemplates({ cwd, agentDir, promptPaths, includeDefaults: false })`; called `expandPromptTemplate(...)` for each input below; ran the script over the **current PR head** (`0710bb1a086`) and, for the before column, over the `origin/main` parser source with the rest of the path identical.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live output from running the real `expandPromptTemplate` over the on-disk templates on the current PR head `0710bb1a086`:

  ```
  TEMPLATES LOADED: [ 'cmd', 'review' ]
  USER TYPES : /cmd don't it's broken now
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=[don't] ARG2=[it's] ARG3=[broken] ARG4=[now]

  USER TYPES : /cmd --title="two words" next
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=[--title=two words] ARG2=[next] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

  USER TYPES : /cmd José's don't fail
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=[José's] ARG2=[don't] ARG3=[fail] ARG4=[]

  USER TYPES : /cmd 'review don't fail
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=['review] ARG2=[don't] ARG3=[fail] ARG4=[]
  ```

- Observed result after fix: apostrophes survive end to end across contractions, multiple contractions, non-ASCII prose, and an unmatched leading quote; mid-token shell quoting (`--title="two words"`) still groups into a single argument. The model receives the exact instruction the user typed.
- What was not tested: the separate native-channel `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.ts` `parseCommandArgs` (a different function, untouched); a fully wired gateway round-trip through a live provider (the proof drives the exact expansion function that produces the user message rather than issuing a model call).
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: none specific — the expansion is deterministic and reproduced on demand.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the same script over the `origin/main` parser (unpatched) corrupts every case except the already-balanced mid-token span:

  ```
  TEMPLATES LOADED: [ 'cmd', 'review' ]
  USER TYPES : /cmd don't it's broken now
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=[dont its] ARG2=[broken] ARG3=[now] ARG4=[]

  USER TYPES : /cmd --title="two words" next
  MODEL GETS : ARG1=[--title=two words] ARG2=[next] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

  USER TYPES : /cmd José's don't fail

  USER TYPES : /cmd 'review don't fail
  ```

target.title: fix(exec): auto-approve recognized read-only boolean flags on default safe bins
target.body:
## Summary

Default safe bins (`cut`, `head`, `tail`, `tr`, `uniq`, `wc`) exist to auto-approve **stdin-only text filters** without a manual approval prompt. But the short-option validator only had an *accept* path for **value-consuming** flags: `consumeShortOptionClusterToken` skipped any flag not in `allowedValueFlags` and, if the cluster contained no value flag, fell through to a terminal `return -1` (reject). So a cluster of pure **boolean** short flags was never auto-approved.

Net effect: the most common read-only invocations of these bins — `wc -l`, `wc -w`, `tr -d`, `tr -s`, `uniq -c` (and `sort -n`/`-r` for operators who add `sort`) — were force-routed to manual approval, even though the bins are stdin-only and every dangerous flag is already in `deniedFlags`. The presence of short flags in `deniedFlags` (`sort: -o, -T`; `jq: -L, -f`; `grep: -f, -r, -R`) shows the intended model is a denylist over recognized flags, so rejecting legitimate boolean flags is an oversight, not a deliberate narrowing.

## Fix

Add an `allowedBooleanFlags` allowlist to the safe-bin profile model and populate it for the six default bins with their read-only, no-side-effect boolean flags:

- `wc`: `-l -w -c -m -L` (+ long forms)
- `tr`: `-d -s -c -C -t`
- `uniq`: `-c -d -u -i -z`
- `cut`: `-s -n -z --complement --only-delimited`
- `head` / `tail`: `-q -v -z` (+ `--quiet/--silent/--verbose/--zero-terminated`). `tail -f`/`-F`/`--follow`/`--retry` are deliberately **excluded** so the auto-approve path can never stream/hang.

The validator now treats a recognized boolean flag as "no value, keep scanning" and accepts a cluster made entirely of recognized boolean flags, while an **unrecognized** short flag still hits `return -1` (fail-closed). The long-option path needs no logic change: feeding boolean long flags into `knownLongFlags` makes `consumeLongOptionToken`'s existing `if (!expectsValue) return index + 1` branch reachable for the first time. Denied flags are still checked first, so `sort -o`, `wc --files0-from`, etc. remain rejected.

This is intentionally **not** exposed as a new `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles` config field (root `AGENTS.md`: the config/env surface bar is high). It fixes the curated built-in default-bin profiles only; custom user profiles keep the existing `allowedValueFlags` / `deniedFlags` model. `sort`/`grep`/`jq` are not default safe bins and are left for a follow-up.

## Behavior change

| Invocation | before | after |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `wc -l`, `wc -w`, `wc --lines` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `tr -d abc`, `tr -s ' '` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `uniq -c`, `cut -s -f 1` | needs approval | **auto-approved** |
| `tr -S a b` (unknown short flag) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |
| `sort -o out.txt` (denied) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |
| `wc --files0-from=list` (denied) | needs approval | needs approval (unchanged) |

## Diff size

```
 docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md       |  4 +-
 src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts  | 32 +++++++++++++
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts  | 73 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++-
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-validator.ts | 23 +++++----
 src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts      | 30 ++++++++++++
 5 files changed, 152 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)
```

## Test plan

- [x] New validator unit tests (`exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts`): boolean short flags accepted (`wc -l`, `-lw`, `uniq -c`, `tr -d`), boolean long flags + abbreviations (`wc --lines`, `--word`), value-on-boolean rejected (`wc --lines=5`), unknown short flags rejected (`tr -S`, `wc -Z`), mixed boolean+value cluster preserved (`uniq -cf 2`).
- [x] New end-to-end-gate tests (`exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts`) through `isSafeBinUsage`: `wc -l`, `wc --words`, `uniq -c`, `tr -d abc` auto-allowed; the existing `tr -S a b` -> rejected guard kept.
- [x] Full `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` + `exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts`: 155 passed (incl. denied-flag matrix and docs-parity).
- [x] `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` clean for the changed files; `oxlint` clean on changed files.
- [ ] CI + clawsweeper re-review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** default safe bins are documented as auto-approved stdin-only text filters, but the safe-bin argv validator rejected pure boolean short-flag clusters, so `wc -l`, `tr -d`, `uniq -c`, `sort -n` were routed to manual approval. After the patch the real auto-approve gate (`isSafeBinUsage`) returns `true` for those recognized read-only boolean forms, while unknown/denied flags stay fail-closed.

**Real environment tested:** Linux 6.8.0-71-generic, Node v22.19, real repo source at branch head `34cef99a` vs `origin/main` (`ec6ad888`), driving the **real exported** `isSafeBinUsage` / `normalizeSafeBins` from `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` via `tsx` — not a stub or a re-implementation. Resolutions use `/usr/bin/<bin>` (a default trusted dir) so the full path/trust/profile gate runs.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `tsx` over a harness that imports the real `isSafeBinUsage` and calls it for each argv, first on this branch (after) then with the two prod files reset to `origin/main` (before):

```
node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/safebin-proof.ts          # after = branch head
git checkout origin/main -- <profiles> <validator>   # revert prod files only
node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/safebin-proof.ts          # before = origin/main
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
############ BEFORE (origin/main) ############
. wc -l                    -> needs approval
. wc -w                    -> needs approval
. wc --lines               -> needs approval
. tr -d abc                -> needs approval
. tr -s                    -> needs approval
. uniq -c                  -> needs approval
. cut -s -f 1              -> needs approval
. tr -S a b                -> needs approval
. sort -o out.txt          -> needs approval
. wc --files0-from=list    -> needs approval

############ AFTER (this branch) ############
> wc -l                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> wc -w                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> wc --lines               -> AUTO-APPROVED
> tr -d abc                -> AUTO-APPROVED
> tr -s                    -> AUTO-APPROVED
> uniq -c                  -> AUTO-APPROVED
> cut -s -f 1              -> AUTO-APPROVED
. tr -S a b                -> needs approval
. sort -o out.txt          -> needs approval
. wc --files0-from=list    -> needs approval
```

**Observed result after fix:** every recognized read-only boolean form flipped from *needs approval* to *AUTO-APPROVED*; the unknown short flag (`tr -S`) and the denied flags (`sort -o`, `wc --files0-from`) stayed fail-closed. Matches the new unit/gate tests.

**What was not tested:** a full gateway + live agent exec round-trip (model issues `wc -l` -> no approval prompt) — proof was taken at the real `isSafeBinUsage` decision boundary, which is the single gate that turns a safe-bin invocation into an auto-approval. No Windows path (safe bins are disabled on Windows by design).
